examples.explanation: n = 6. Initialize count = 0. Loop i from 1 to 6: i=1: 6 % 1 = 0 → count = 1. i=2: 6 % 2 = 0 → count = 2. i=3: 6 % 3 = 0 → count = 3. i=4: 6 % 4 = 2 ≠ 0 → no change. i=5: 6 % 5 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=6: 6 % 6 = 0 → count = 4. Return count = 4.

n = 13. Initialize count = 0. Loop i from 1 to 13: i=1: 13 % 1 = 0 → count = 1. i=2: 13 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=3: 13 % 3 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=4: 13 % 4 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=5: 13 % 5 = 3 ≠ 0 → no change. i=6: 13 % 6 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=7: 13 % 7 = 6 ≠ 0 → no change. i=8: 13 % 8 = 5 ≠ 0 → no change. i=9: 13 % 9 = 4 ≠ 0 → no change. i=10: 13 % 10 = 3 ≠ 0 → no change. i=11: 13 % 11 = 2 ≠ 0 → no change. i=12: 13 % 12 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=13: 13 % 13 = 0 → count = 2. Return count = 2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate from 1 to n, checking if each integer divides n evenly, and count the divisors. This is straightforward but inefficient for large n.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Divisors Using Brute Force O(n)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of counting all divisors of n by checking every integer from 1 to n.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to counting divisors is based on the fundamental definition of divisibility: a number d is a divisor of n if n divided by d leaves no remainder. This approach systematically tests every integer from 1 to n, incrementing a counter whenever the division condition is satisfied. The intuition is straightforward: since any divisor must be between 1 and n inclusive (as divisors cannot exceed the number itself), checking all possibilities guarantees we find every divisor. This is a direct translation of the mathematical definition into an algorithm. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a counter variable to 0. 2. Loop through integers i from 1 to n inclusive. 3. For each i, check if n modulo i equals 0 (n % i == 0). 4. If true, increment the counter. 5. After the loop, return the counter. Time complexity is O(n) because we perform n iterations, each with constant-time operations (modulo check and increment). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a fixed amount of extra memory for the counter and loop variable. This connects to the overall problem by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in the loop range (should include n), forgetting to handle edge cases like n=0 or n=1, and using integer division instead of modulo for the divisibility check. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def count_divisors(n): count = 0; for i in range(1, n+1): if n % i == 0: count += 1; return count.

examples.trace: n = 6. Initialize count = 0. Loop i from 1 to 6: i=1: 6 % 1 = 0 → count = 1. i=2: 6 % 2 = 0 → count = 2. i=3: 6 % 3 = 0 → count = 3. i=4: 6 % 4 = 2 ≠ 0 → no change. i=5: 6 % 5 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=6: 6 % 6 = 0 → count = 4. Return count = 4.

n = 13. Initialize count = 0. Loop i from 1 to 13: i=1: 13 % 1 = 0 → count = 1. i=2: 13 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=3: 13 % 3 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=4: 13 % 4 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=5: 13 % 5 = 3 ≠ 0 → no change. i=6: 13 % 6 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=7: 13 % 7 = 6 ≠ 0 → no change. i=8: 13 % 8 = 5 ≠ 0 → no change. i=9: 13 % 9 = 4 ≠ 0 → no change. i=10: 13 % 10 = 3 ≠ 0 → no change. i=11: 13 % 11 = 2 ≠ 0 → no change. i=12: 13 % 12 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=13: 13 % 13 = 0 → count = 2. Return count = 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Divisibility for a Single Number
ladders.desc: Determine if a given integer d divides another integer n evenly.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core operation in the brute force approach: checking whether one number divides another without leaving a remainder. The intuition is based on the definition of divisibility in mathematics: d divides n if there exists an integer k such that n = d × k. In computational terms, this is equivalent to n modulo d equaling 0. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Take two integers n and d as input. 2. Compute the remainder when n is divided by d (using the modulo operator %). 3. If the remainder is 0, return True (d is a divisor); otherwise, return False. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves a single arithmetic operation. Space complexity is O(1) since no extra data structures are used. This connects to the level above (L0) by being the condition checked in each iteration of the loop. Common mistakes include: confusing modulo with integer division, not handling d=0 (which would cause division by zero; in this context, d ranges from 1 to n, so it's safe), and incorrect comparison (e.g., using n/d == integer instead of n % d == 0). Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def is_divisor(n, d): return n % d == 0.

examples.trace: n = 10, d = 3. Compute 10 % 3 = 1. Since 1 ≠ 0, return False.
n = 10, d = 5. Compute 10 % 5 = 0. Since 0 == 0, return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Through All Possible Divisors
ladders.desc: Loop through integers from 1 to n to test each as a potential divisor.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves systematically generating all integers in the range [1, n] and applying the divisibility check to each. The intuition is that any divisor of n must lie within this range because a divisor cannot be greater than the number itself (for positive integers). By exhaustively checking every candidate, we ensure no divisor is missed. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a counter to 0. 2. For each integer i from 1 to n inclusive: a. Check if i divides n (using the modulo operation). b. If yes, increment the counter. 3. Return the counter. Time complexity is O(n) due to the loop running n times. Space complexity is O(1) as only a few variables are used. This connects to L1 by using the divisibility check within the loop. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (e.g., looping to n-1 instead of n), incorrect loop initialization, and modifying the loop variable incorrectly. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: for i in range(1, n+1): if n % i == 0: count += 1.

examples.trace: Initialize count = 0. Loop i from 1 to 4: i=1: 4 % 1 = 0 → count = 1. i=2: 4 % 2 = 0 → count = 2. i=3: 4 % 3 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=4: 4 % 4 = 0 → count = 3. Return count = 3.

Initialize count = 0. Loop i from 1 to 9: i=1: 9 % 1 = 0 → count = 1. i=2: 9 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=3: 9 % 3 = 0 → count = 2. i=4: 9 % 4 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=5: 9 % 5 = 4 ≠ 0 → no change. i=6: 9 % 6 = 3 ≠ 0 → no change. i=7: 9 % 7 = 2 ≠ 0 → no change. i=8: 9 % 8 = 1 ≠ 0 → no change. i=9: 9 % 9 = 0 → count = 3. Return count = 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation for Remainder
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation is fundamental to checking divisibility. It returns the remainder of the division of two integers. The intuition is that if dividing n by d leaves no remainder, then d divides n evenly. Mathematically, n % d = n - d * floor(n/d). In programming, it's a built-in operator. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Take two integers a and b (where b ≠ 0). 2. Compute a % b, which gives the remainder when a is divided by b. 3. The result is an integer between 0 and |b|-1. Time complexity is O(1) as it's a basic arithmetic operation. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by being the operation used in the divisibility check. Common mistakes include: misunderstanding modulo with negative numbers (in this problem, we assume non-negative inputs), confusing it with division, and not handling b=0 (which is undefined). Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: remainder = a % b.

examples.trace: Compute 10 % 3. 10 divided by 3 is 3 with remainder 1. So, 10 % 3 = 1.
Compute 15 % 5. 15 divided by 5 is 3 with remainder 0. So, 15 % 5 = 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Comparison
ladders.desc: Perform division on integers and compare values to check for equality.
ladders.explanation: This level involves basic arithmetic and comparison operations that underpin the modulo check. Integer division yields the quotient, and comparison checks if two values are equal. The intuition is that to check if d divides n, we can compute n/d and see if it's an integer, but in programming, it's more efficient to use modulo. However, understanding division is key. The step-by-step algorithm for a simplified check: 1. Compute the quotient q = n // d (integer division). 2. Compute the product p = d * q. 3. Check if p == n. If true, d divides n. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as modulo is related to division (remainder = n - d * (n//d)). Common mistakes include: using floating-point division instead of integer division, which can lead to precision errors, and incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: q = n // d; if d * q == n: return True else: return False.

examples.trace: Compute q = 10 // 3 = 3. Compute p = 3 * 3 = 9. Check if 9 == 10? No, so return False.
Compute q = 10 // 5 = 2. Compute p = 5 * 2 = 10. Check if 10 == 10? Yes, so return True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Integers and Loops
ladders.desc: Work with integer variables and basic for-loops to iterate a fixed number of times.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves handling integer data types and using loops to repeat operations. Integers are whole numbers, and in programming, they are used for counting and indexing. A for-loop allows executing a block of code multiple times, which is essential for checking each potential divisor. The intuition is that to test many values, we use a loop instead of writing repetitive code. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Define an integer n (e.g., n = 5). 2. Use a for-loop to iterate from 1 to n. 3. In each iteration, print or process the current value. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L4 as loops are used to apply operations like division repeatedly. Common mistakes include: incorrect loop bounds (e.g., starting from 0 instead of 1), misunderstanding loop variables, and infinite loops. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: for i in range(1, n+1): print(i).

examples.trace: Set n = 3. Loop i from 1 to 3: i=1 → print 1. i=2 → print 2. i=3 → print 3. Output: 1, 2, 3.
Set n = 5. Loop i from 1 to 5: i=1 → print 1. i=2 → print 2. i=3 → print 3. i=4 → print 4. i=5 → print 5. Output: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

approaches.name: Optimized Iteration O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Iterate only up to the square root of n, counting divisors in pairs (i and n/i) for each divisor found. This reduces the search space significantly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Divisors Using Optimized Iteration
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting all divisors of n by iterating up to sqrt(n) and counting in pairs.
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete solution to count all divisors of a given integer n using the optimized iteration approach. The key insight is that divisors come in pairs (i, n/i), except when i equals n/i (perfect square case). By iterating only up to the square root of n, we reduce the time complexity from O(n) to O(√n). The algorithm works by initializing a count to 0, then looping i from 1 to floor(√n). For each i that divides n evenly (n % i == 0), we increment count by 2 (for the pair i and n/i). However, if i equals n/i (i*i == n), we only increment by 1 to avoid double-counting the same divisor. This approach efficiently finds all divisors because if i divides n, then n/i must also divide n, forming a divisor pair. The space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to L1's divisor counting subroutine by wrapping it in the complete problem context. Common mistakes include forgetting the perfect square case (which would double-count the square root divisor), using floating-point sqrt (which can cause precision issues), or not handling n=0 or n=1 correctly. Always use integer sqrt and handle edge cases explicitly.

Algorithm:
1. If n <= 0, return 0 (or handle as per problem constraints)
2. Initialize count = 0
3. For i from 1 to floor(sqrt(n)):
   a. If n % i == 0:
      i. If i == n/i: count += 1
      ii. Else: count += 2
4. Return count

Time: O(√n), Space: O(1)

Python-style pseudocode:
def count_divisors(n):
    if n <= 0: return 0
    count = 0
    i = 1
    while i*i <= n:
        if n % i == 0:
            if i == n//i:
                count += 1
            else:
                count += 2
        i += 1
    return count

examples.trace:
n=12, sqrt(12)≈3.46, floor=3
Initialize count=0
i=1: 12%1==0, i=1, n/i=12, 1≠12 → count+=2 → count=2
i=2: 12%2==0, i=2, n/i=6, 2≠6 → count+=2 → count=4
i=3: 12%3==0, i=3, n/i=4, 3≠4 → count+=2 → count=6
i=4: 4*4=16>12, loop ends
Return count=6
Divisors: 1,2,3,4,6,12

n=16, sqrt(16)=4, floor=4
Initialize count=0
i=1: 16%1==0, i=1, n/i=16, 1≠16 → count+=2 → count=2
i=2: 16%2==0, i=2, n/i=8, 2≠8 → count+=2 → count=4
i=3: 16%3!=0 → no change
i=4: 16%4==0, i=4, n/i=4, 4==4 → count+=1 → count=5
Return count=5
Divisors: 1,2,4,8,16

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Divisors Up to Square Root
ladders.desc: Implement the core divisor counting logic that iterates up to sqrt(n) and counts divisor pairs.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the optimized approach. It takes an integer n and counts how many divisors it has by only checking numbers up to the square root of n. The intuition is based on the mathematical property that divisors always come in pairs (d, n/d). If d divides n, then n/d must also divide n. By checking only up to √n, we ensure we encounter each divisor pair exactly once (except when d = n/d, which happens only when n is a perfect square). For each i from 1 to √n, if i divides n, we count both i and n/i as divisors. However, when i = n/i (i² = n), we count it only once to avoid duplication. This reduces the number of iterations dramatically compared to checking all numbers up to n. The time complexity is O(√n) and space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the complete counting logic that L0 wraps with edge case handling. Common mistakes include using floating-point sqrt (use integer sqrt via i*i <= n), forgetting the perfect square case, or incorrectly incrementing count. Always test with perfect squares like 9, 16, 25.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize count = 0
2. For i from 1 to floor(sqrt(n)):
   a. If n % i == 0:
      i. If i == n/i: count += 1
      ii. Else: count += 2
3. Return count

Time: O(√n), Space: O(1)

Python-style pseudocode:
def count_divisors_sqrt(n):
    count = 0
    i = 1
    while i*i <= n:
        if n % i == 0:
            if i == n//i:
                count += 1
            else:
                count += 2
        i += 1
    return count

examples.trace:
n=9, sqrt(9)=3, floor=3
count=0
i=1: 9%1==0, i=1, n/i=9, 1≠9 → count+=2 → count=2
i=2: 9%2!=0 → no change
i=3: 9%3==0, i=3, n/i=3, 3==3 → count+=1 → count=3
Return count=3
Divisors: 1,3,9

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisor Pair Property and Square Root Bound
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the mathematical insight that divisors come in pairs and we only need to check up to sqrt(n).
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the central mathematical insight that makes the optimization possible. For any positive integer n, if d is a divisor of n, then n/d is also a divisor of n. This forms a divisor pair (d, n/d). Notice that in each pair, one divisor is ≤ √n and the other is ≥ √n. This is because if both were > √n, their product would exceed n, and if both were < √n, their product would be less than n. Therefore, by checking all possible divisors from 1 to √n, we can capture all divisor pairs. When we find a divisor i ≤ √n, we automatically get its pair n/i. The only special case is when i = n/i, which happens when i² = n (n is a perfect square). In that case, i and n/i are the same number, so we count it once. This insight reduces the search space from n to √n, giving O(√n) time complexity. This feeds into L1 by providing the theoretical foundation for the iteration limit. Common mistakes include not understanding why the square root is the boundary, or incorrectly assuming all divisors are found by just checking up to n/2. Visualize with examples: for n=12, √12≈3.46, divisors are (1,12), (2,6), (3,4) – note first element of each pair ≤3.46.

Algorithm concept:
1. For each i from 1 to √n:
   a. If i divides n, then i and n/i are both divisors
   b. Count them appropriately (2 for distinct pairs, 1 if i=n/i)

Time: O(√n), Space: O(1)

Python-style pseudocode illustrating the logic:
# This demonstrates the pair property
for i in range(1, int(sqrt(n))+1):
    if n % i == 0:
        print(f"Divisor pair: ({i}, {n//i})")

examples.trace:
n=25, sqrt(25)=5, check i=1 to 5
i=1: 25%1==0 → pair (1,25)
i=2: 25%2!=0 → no pair
i=3: 25%3!=0 → no pair
i=4: 25%4!=0 → no pair
i=5: 25%5==0 → pair (5,5) but same number
Total divisors: 3 (1,5,25)

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Square Root and Division Check
ladders.desc: Compute integer square root and check divisibility efficiently.
ladders.explanation:
This level combines two fundamental operations needed for the optimized approach: computing the integer square root and checking divisibility. The integer square root of n is the largest integer i such that i² ≤ n. We need this to determine our iteration limit. Instead of using floating-point sqrt() which can have precision issues, we use integer arithmetic: loop while i*i ≤ n. This ensures we check exactly up to floor(√n). Simultaneously, we need to check if i divides n evenly using the modulo operator (n % i == 0). If true, i is a divisor. These two operations work together: for each i from 1 to √n, we check if i divides n. The time complexity for this building block is O(√n) for the loop, with O(1) operations per iteration. Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanical steps to implement the divisor pair insight. Common mistakes include using floating-point sqrt (which may give incorrect bounds for large n), not using integer division for n/i, or inefficient divisibility checks. Always use integer operations and ensure the loop condition is i*i <= n.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize i = 1
2. While i*i <= n:
   a. If n % i == 0: process divisor i
   b. i += 1

Time: O(√n), Space: O(1)

Python-style pseudocode:
def find_divisors_up_to_sqrt(n):
    divisors = []
    i = 1
    while i*i <= n:
        if n % i == 0:
            divisors.append(i)  # i ≤ √n
        i += 1
    return divisors

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Use modulo (%) to check divisibility and integer division (//) to compute quotient.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the basic arithmetic operations required: modulo (%) and integer division (//). The modulo operator a % b returns the remainder when a is divided by b. If n % i == 0, then i divides n evenly, meaning i is a divisor of n. This is our divisibility test. Integer division a // b returns the quotient as an integer (floor division). When we find a divisor i, we need its pair n//i. These operations are fundamental because they allow us to identify divisors and compute their pairs efficiently. Understanding these operations is crucial for implementing the algorithm correctly. Time and space complexity for these operations are O(1) as they are basic arithmetic. This feeds into L3 by providing the tools to check divisibility and compute quotients within the square root loop. Common mistakes include confusing / (float division) with // (integer division), or not understanding that modulo with 0 is undefined. Always ensure i > 0 when using n % i. Practice with examples: 10 % 3 = 1 (not divisible), 10 % 2 = 0 (divisible), 10 // 3 = 3, 10 // 2 = 5.

Algorithm steps:
1. To check if i divides n: if n % i == 0
2. To get the pair divisor: pair = n // i

Time: O(1) per operation, Space: O(1)

Python-style pseudocode:
def check_divisibility(n, i):
    return n % i == 0

def get_pair(n, i):
    return n // i

examples.trace:
Check divisibility: 15 % 3 = 0 → True, so 3 divides 15
Compute pair: 15 // 3 = 5
So divisor pair is (3,5)

Check divisibility: 15 % 4 = 3 ≠ 0 → False, so 4 is not a divisor
Compute pair (if it were): 15 // 4 = 3 (but since not divisible, this isn't a divisor pair)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Divisor?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic definition of a divisor and how to list them.
ladders.explanation:
This is the foundational concept: a divisor of an integer n is any integer d such that when n is divided by d, the remainder is 0. In other words, d divides n evenly. For example, divisors of 6 are 1, 2, 3, and 6 because 6 ÷ 1 = 6, 6 ÷ 2 = 3, 6 ÷ 3 = 2, 6 ÷ 6 = 1 all have remainder 0. Every positive integer n has at least two divisors: 1 and n itself. To find all divisors, the naive approach is to check every integer from 1 to n and test if it divides n. This gives O(n) time complexity. The intuition for the optimized approach starts here: we realize we don't need to check all numbers because divisors come in pairs. But at this level, we focus on understanding what a divisor is and how to identify them using the modulo operation. This feeds into L4 by establishing why we use modulo to check divisibility. Common mistakes include confusing divisors with multiples, or forgetting that divisors must be integers. Always remember: if n % d == 0, then d is a divisor of n.

Algorithm (naive):
1. Initialize an empty list of divisors
2. For d from 1 to n:
   a. If n % d == 0, add d to the list
3. Return the list

Time: O(n), Space: O(d) where d is number of divisors

Python-style pseudocode:
def list_divisors_naive(n):
    divisors = []
    for d in range(1, n+1):
        if n % d == 0:
            divisors.append(d)
    return divisors

examples.trace:
n=6
Check d=1: 6%1=0 → add 1
Check d=2: 6%2=0 → add 2
Check d=3: 6%3=0 → add 3
Check d=4: 6%4=2 ≠0 → skip
Check d=5: 6%5=1 ≠0 → skip
Check d=6: 6%6=0 → add 6
Result: [1,2,3,6]

n=9
Check d=1: 9%1=0 → add 1
Check d=2: 9%2=1 ≠0 → skip
Check d=3: 9%3=0 → add 3
Check d=4: 9%4=1 ≠0 → skip
Check d=5: 9%5=4 ≠0 → skip
Check d=6: 9%6=3 ≠0 → skip
Check d=7: 9%7=2 ≠0 → skip
Check d=8: 9%8=1 ≠0 → skip
Check d=9: 9%9=0 → add 9
Result: [1,3,9]

approaches.name: Prime Factorization O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Factorize n into its prime factors, then compute the divisor count using the formula based on exponents. This method focuses on prime decomposition rather than direct divisor checking.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Divisors via Prime Factorization
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting divisors using prime factorization with sqrt(n) optimization
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to count all divisors of a given integer n using prime factorization. The intuition is that any divisor of n can be expressed as a product of the prime factors of n, where each prime factor's exponent ranges from 0 to its maximum in n's factorization. The divisor count formula (e₁+1)(e₂+1)...(eₖ+1) emerges from this combinatorial principle. The algorithm iterates from 2 to sqrt(n), dividing n by each prime factor found while counting its exponent. After the loop, if n > 1, it means n itself is prime (or the last prime factor). The total divisor count is computed by multiplying (exponent+1) for each prime factor. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) because we only check up to sqrt(n) - any factor larger than sqrt(n) would have a corresponding smaller factor already found. Space is O(1) as we only store a few variables. This connects to L1 by using the prime factorization subroutine as its core component. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle the case when n > 1 after the loop (meaning n itself is prime), integer overflow with large n, and not optimizing to sqrt(n). Real code pattern: def count_divisors(n): result = 1; i = 2; while i*i <= n: if n%i == 0: count = 0; while n%i == 0: n //= i; count += 1; result *= (count+1); i += 1; if n > 1: result *= 2; return result.

examples.trace: n=28, result=1, i=2, 2*2=4<=28, 28%2=0, count=0, while n%2=0: n=28//2=14, count=1; n=14//2=7, count=2; n=7%2=1≠0, exit inner while; result=1*(2+1)=3; i=3, 3*3=9<=7? No, exit outer while; n=7>1, result=3*2=6; return 6. Divisors: 1,2,4,7,14,28.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prime Factorization with Exponent Counting
ladders.desc: Factorize n into prime factors and record their exponents
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine decomposes a number n into its prime factors while counting the exponent of each prime. The intuition is that every integer >1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). By iterating from 2 upward and dividing n whenever divisible, we extract prime factors in increasing order. The sqrt(n) optimization works because if n has a factor f > sqrt(n), then it must have a corresponding factor n/f < sqrt(n) already processed. The algorithm: initialize empty list/dict for factors; for i from 2 to sqrt(n): while n%i==0: record i as factor with count++; n//=i; if n>1: record n as prime factor with exponent 1. Time O(sqrt(n)), space O(k) where k is number of distinct primes. This feeds into L0 by providing the factorization needed for divisor count formula. Common mistakes: not using while loop to fully extract each prime factor, missing the final n>1 case, and inefficiently checking all numbers instead of only primes (but checking all is simpler and still O(sqrt(n))). Real code pattern: def prime_factors(n): factors={}; i=2; while i*i<=n: while n%i==0: factors[i]=factors.get(i,0)+1; n//=i; i+=1; if n>1: factors[n]=factors.get(n,0)+1; return factors.

examples.trace: n=28, factors={}, i=2, 2*2=4<=28, while n%2=0: factors[2]=1, n=14; n%2=0: factors[2]=2, n=7; n%2=1≠0, exit inner while; i=3, 9<=7? No, exit outer while; n=7>1, factors[7]=1; return {2:2,7:1}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisor Count Formula from Prime Exponents
ladders.desc: Compute divisor count using (e₁+1)(e₂+1)... formula given prime exponents
ladders.explanation: This core logic computes the total number of divisors given the prime factorization of a number. The intuition is combinatorial: for each prime factor p with exponent e, a divisor can include p⁰, p¹, ..., pᵉ (e+1 choices). Since divisors are formed by independent choices across different primes, the total count is the product of (e+1) for all primes. For example, if n = 2²×3¹, divisors are: 2⁰3⁰=1, 2¹3⁰=2, 2²3⁰=4, 2⁰3¹=3, 2¹3¹=6, 2²3¹=12 → 6 divisors = (2+1)(1+1). The algorithm: given dictionary of prime→exponent, initialize result=1; for each exponent e in values: result *= (e+1); return result. Time O(k) where k is number of distinct primes, space O(1). This connects to L1 by taking its output (prime exponents) as input. Common mistakes: multiplying exponents instead of (e+1), forgetting to initialize result as 1, and integer overflow with large results. Real code pattern: def count_from_exponents(exp_dict): result=1; for e in exp_dict.values(): result*=(e+1); return result.

examples.trace: exp_dict={2:2,7:1}, result=1, for e=2: result=1*(2+1)=3, for e=1: result=3*(1+1)=6, return 6.
exp_dict={2:2,5:2}, result=1, for e=2: result=1*3=3, for e=2: result=3*3=9, return 9.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Division to Extract Factors
ladders.desc: Repeatedly divide n by a factor while divisible to extract all occurrences
ladders.explanation: This building block repeatedly divides n by a given factor i as long as i divides n evenly. The intuition is that if i is a factor of n, then n can be expressed as i×m, and by dividing repeatedly we extract all powers of i from n. This is crucial for prime factorization to get the full exponent of each prime. The algorithm: while n%i == 0: n = n//i; count++ (optional). For example, for n=72 and i=2: 72%2=0 → n=36, count=1; 36%2=0 → n=18, count=2; 18%2=0 → n=9, count=3; 9%2=1≠0 → stop. Time O(log_i(n)) in worst case, but overall bounded. Space O(1). This feeds into L1 as the inner loop for each potential prime factor. Common mistakes: using floating-point division instead of integer division, not updating n correctly, and infinite loops if condition is wrong. Real code pattern: while n%i == 0: n //= i; count += 1.

examples.trace: Start: n=72, i=2, count=0. Check 72%2=0: true, n=72//2=36, count=1. Check 36%2=0: true, n=36//2=18, count=2. Check 18%2=0: true, n=18//2=9, count=3. Check 9%2=1≠0: false, exit. Final n=9, count=3.
Start: n=125, i=5, count=0. Check 125%5=0: true, n=125//5=25, count=1. Check 25%5=0: true, n=25//5=5, count=2. Check 5%5=0: true, n=5//5=1, count=3. Check 1%5=1≠0: false, exit. Final n=1, count=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Divisibility and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Determine if a divides b and perform integer division
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two fundamental arithmetic tasks: checking if integer a divides integer b (b%a==0) and performing integer division (b//a). The intuition is that divisibility means b can be expressed as a×k for some integer k, with no remainder. The modulo operator % gives the remainder, so b%a==0 indicates divisibility. Integer division // gives the quotient, discarding any remainder. These operations are essential for extracting factors in prime factorization. For example, for a=3, b=12: 12%3=0 → divisible; 12//3=4 → quotient. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L3 as the condition and update in the while loop. Common mistakes: confusing / (float division) with // (integer division), not handling division by zero, and misunderstanding modulo with negative numbers (but here we deal with positives). Real code pattern: if b%a == 0: quotient = b//a.

examples.trace: Check divisibility: compute 12%3=0, since 0==0, divisible=True. Compute quotient: 12//3=4, so quotient=4.
Check divisibility: compute 17%5=2, since 2≠0, divisible=False. Compute quotient anyway: 17//5=3 (integer division truncates toward zero), so quotient=3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Factors
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers, factors, and basic number properties
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the simplest prerequisite concepts: prime numbers, factors, and basic properties of integers. A prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself (e.g., 2,3,5,7). A factor (or divisor) of an integer n is an integer that divides n without leaving a remainder (e.g., factors of 12 are 1,2,3,4,6,12). Every integer >1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime numbers (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). For example, 12=2×2×3=2²×3. The intuition is that primes are the building blocks of numbers, and factors are combinations of these primes. This is essential for L4 because divisibility checks rely on these definitions. Common mistakes: considering 1 as prime (it's not, as it has only one divisor), or confusing multiples with factors. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n<2: return False; for i in range(2,int(n**0.5)+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Check if 7 is prime: divisors are 1 and 7 only, so yes, it's prime. Factors: numbers that divide 7 evenly: 1 and 7.
Check if 12 is prime: it has divisors 1,2,3,4,6,12 (more than two), so not prime. Factors: all numbers that divide 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12.
